summary:
The French Revolutionary Wars (1792–1802 AD) were the series of conflicts in which Revolutionary France fought the monarchies of Europe — Austria, Prussia, Britain, Spain, the Netherlands, Sardinia, and others — in defense of the Revolution and, increasingly, in pursuit of French territorial expansion and ideological hegemony. The wars began when France declared war on Austria in April 1792 (ostensibly to spread revolution but also to consolidate the Revolutionary government's domestic position) and rapidly escalated after the [[event:2481|execution of Louis XVI]] into a broad coalition against France. The French Revolutionary armies, fueled by nationalist idealism, the levée en masse (universal conscription, 1793), and the talent of a new generation of officers promoted on merit rather than birth, proved militarily formidable — defeating the professional armies of Europe repeatedly.

The wars transformed European warfare and politics fundamentally. The levée en masse — France's mobilization of the entire nation for war — introduced modern total war, with the nation rather than merely its professional army as the military instrument. French armies spread revolutionary ideas across Europe: the abolition of feudalism, equal rights before the law, and the principle of popular sovereignty followed French conquests into the Netherlands, Switzerland, Italy, and Germany, undermining the old regime everywhere. The wars produced [[event:3316|Napoleon's Italian campaign]] (1796–97) that made him a national hero, then his [[event:3317|Egyptian campaign]] (1798–99), and ultimately the coup that brought Napoleon to power (1799). The Peace of Amiens (1802) briefly ended the wars before the Napoleonic Wars resumed — ultimately transforming Europe's political map more thoroughly than any conflict since the fall of Rome.
